question: What is a sampling distribution and why is it a critical concept in statistical inference?
expectedPoints: A sampling distribution is the probability distribution of a statistic (e.g., mean, proportion) obtained from a large number of samples drawn from a specific population.
It is different from the population distribution (distribution of individuals) and a single sample distribution.
It is critical because it allows us to quantify the uncertainty of our statistical estimates, forming the basis for constructing confidence intervals and conducting hypothesis tests.

question: Explain the Central Limit Theorem (CLT) and its profound importance for the sampling distribution of the mean.
expectedPoints: The CLT states that for a sufficiently large sample size (n), the sampling distribution of the sample mean will be approximately normally distributed, regardless of the shape of the population distribution.
The mean of this sampling distribution will be equal to the population mean (μ).
The standard deviation of this sampling distribution, called the standard error, is equal to the population standard deviation (σ) divided by the square root of the sample size (√n).
Its importance lies in allowing us to make inferences about a population mean using the properties of the normal distribution, even when the underlying population is not normal.

question: Describe how the sampling distribution of the sample proportion is derived and applied. Use an example of estimating the proportion of faulty lines of code in a large software program to illustrate the process of constructing a confidence interval.
expectedPoints: The sampling distribution for a proportion (p̂) is built by repeatedly taking samples of size n from a population with a true proportion p and calculating p̂ for each sample.
For large n, this distribution is approximately normal (checked using conditions: n*p >= 10 and n*(1-p) >= 10).
Its mean is the population proportion p, and its standard error is √[p(1-p)/n].

Application Example: To estimate the proportion of bugs, a sample of n code lines is taken, yielding a sample proportion p̂. A 95% confidence interval is constructed as: p̂ ± z* √[p̂(1-p̂)/n], where z* is the critical value from the standard normal distribution (1.96). This interval provides a range of plausible values for the true population proportion p.
